name: Seller Disclosure Statement
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Seller Disclosure Statement for residential real estate transactions, ensuring compliance with state and federal requirements. Gathers property details from documents and discloses conditions of structural components, mechanical systems, and known defects. Use this skill when preparing seller disclosures during property sales to protect sellers and inform buyers.
content:
# Seller Disclosure Statement - Professional Legal Workflow

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Seller Disclosure Statement for a real estate transaction. This critical document serves as the seller's legally binding declaration of the property's condition and must comply with all applicable state and federal disclosure requirements. Your role is to create a thorough, accurate disclosure that protects both parties while meeting all jurisdictional requirements.

## Initial Information Gathering and Document Foundation

Begin by searching through any uploaded transaction documents, property records, inspection reports, or correspondence to extract essential property information and identify known issues that require disclosure. Look for the complete legal names of all sellers as they appear on title documents, the full property address with county designation, the assessor's parcel number, legal property description, and any references to prior inspections, repairs, or property conditions. Extract dates of any known defects, repair work, or incidents that must be disclosed. Identify the specific state jurisdiction to ensure compliance with the correct statutory disclosure requirements.

Once you have gathered this foundational information, structure the document header to establish its legal authority. The header must identify all sellers by their full legal names, provide complete property identification including the legal description and APN, and cite the specific state statute mandating seller disclosures. Include a clear statement that the seller is disclosing all known material facts to the best of their knowledge and belief, along with a prominent notice advising buyers of their right to conduct independent inspections and that this disclosure does not substitute for professional property evaluations.

## Comprehensive Structural and Systems Disclosure

Draft detailed disclosures addressing every major structural component and mechanical system within the property. When describing the foundation, go beyond simple acknowledgment of its existence to address any known cracks, settlement patterns, water intrusion history, or past foundation repairs. If documents reference foundation work, include specific details about when repairs occurred, which contractor performed the work, and whether warranties remain in effect. For the roof, disclose its approximate age, composition, any history of leaks or repairs, and remaining warranty coverage if applicable. Be specific about leak locations and dates if this information appears in your document search results.

Address plumbing systems with particularity, identifying pipe materials throughout the property, especially noting the presence of polybutylene, galvanized steel, or other materials that may concern buyers. Disclose any history of leaks, low water pressure, drain problems, or sewer line issues. Clearly state whether the property connects to municipal sewer or operates on a septic system, and if septic, include the date of last pumping and inspection if available in the documents. For electrical systems, identify the service panel amperage, note any aluminum wiring, disclose known code violations or safety concerns, and address the presence or absence of GFCI protection in required locations. Include any history of electrical problems, circuit overloads, or past electrical work.

Provide thorough HVAC disclosures including the age of heating and cooling equipment, fuel types, maintenance history if documented, and any known operational deficiencies. Similarly address water heaters with age, capacity, fuel type, and condition information. Throughout these system disclosures, reference specific inspection reports, contractor evaluations, or repair invoices found in the transaction documents, citing the document name and date to provide buyers with clear source attribution for disclosed information.

## Environmental Hazards and Mandatory Federal Disclosures

Address all environmental conditions and hazards requiring disclosure under federal and state law with the specificity these serious issues demand. For any property constructed before 1978, include the federally mandated lead-based paint disclosure acknowledging whether the seller has knowledge of lead-based paint or lead-based paint hazards in the property. State clearly whether lead-based paint is present, absent, or unknown, and note that the buyer will receive the EPA pamphlet "Protect Your Family from Lead in Your Home" as required by federal law. Include the buyer's right to conduct a lead-based paint inspection during a ten-day period unless the parties negotiate a different timeframe.

Disclose any knowledge of asbestos-containing materials, particularly in insulation, floor tiles, ceiling materials, or roofing components. If asbestos testing or abatement has occurred, reference the specific reports and describe the scope of work completed. Address radon testing by providing actual test results if available in the documents, or acknowledge that no testing has been conducted if that is the case. For flooding history, provide detailed chronological disclosure of any flooding events including the date, source of water intrusion, extent of damage, insurance claims filed, and remediation performed. State clearly whether the property is located within a FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Area and whether flood insurance is required by mortgage lenders.

Disclose the presence of any underground storage tanks, whether currently in use, abandoned in place, or previously removed, along with any soil contamination issues or environmental remediation activities. Address proximity to hazardous waste sites listed on federal or state databases, former industrial uses of the property that may have caused contamination, and any environmental cleanup activities conducted on or near the property. Provide thorough disclosure of any known mold presence, past water damage, or ongoing moisture problems that create conditions conducive to mold growth, including specific locations and any remediation efforts undertaken. For properties in jurisdictions requiring natural hazard disclosures, address earthquake fault zones, seismic hazard areas, wildfire severity zones, and any other geographically specific risks mandated by state law.

## Legal Encumbrances and Title Matters

Provide comprehensive disclosure of all legal matters affecting the property's title, use, and enjoyment. Identify every recorded easement by describing its purpose, location on the property, and practical impact on the owner's use. Distinguish between utility easements that typically run along property boundaries, access easements that may grant neighbors or third parties crossing rights, and conservation easements that restrict development or use. If documents reveal boundary disputes, encroachments, or survey discrepancies, disclose these matters with specificity including the nature of the dispute, parties involved, and current status of resolution efforts.

For properties subject to homeowners association governance, provide the HOA's complete legal name, management company contact information, current monthly or annual assessment amounts, and details of any special assessments that have been approved or are under consideration. Disclose any pending or threatened litigation involving the HOA or the property itself, and note that buyers should receive copies of or access to the CC&Rs, bylaws, articles of incorporation, and recent HOA financial statements. Address any HOA violations, fines, or compliance issues affecting the property.

Disclose the property's current zoning classification and any known non-conforming uses or structures that predate current zoning requirements but may face restrictions on expansion or rebuilding. Identify any building permits pulled for improvements, additions, or alterations, and state whether final inspections were completed and certificates of occupancy obtained. If document searches reveal unpermitted work, disclose this clearly as it may affect the buyer's ability to obtain financing or insurance. Address any code violations, notices of violation, compliance orders, or other governmental enforcement actions, including their current status and any required corrective measures. Disclose rental restrictions, age restrictions, occupancy limitations, or other use restrictions imposed by CC&Rs, local ordinances, or other sources.

## Neighborhood Conditions and Material Facts

Disclose material facts about the surrounding neighborhood and area that could affect a reasonable buyer's decision to purchase the property or the price they would be willing to pay. Address known nuisances including noise from nearby airports, highways, railroads, commercial operations, industrial facilities, or other sources that impact the property's quiet enjoyment. Disclose proximity to commercial or industrial facilities, waste treatment plants, agricultural operations using pesticides, or other land uses that may affect air quality, odors, or quality of life.

Include information about planned public improvements such as road widening projects, new highway construction, utility installations, or major development proposals that could affect traffic patterns, property access, or neighborhood character. If documents reference upcoming special assessments for public improvements, disclose these clearly with estimated costs and timing. Address any known neighborhood safety concerns to the extent permitted and required by state law, recognizing that disclosure requirements vary significantly by jurisdiction regarding crime statistics, registered sex offenders, and similar matters.

Disclose any deaths that occurred on the property if required by your state's disclosure statute, noting that requirements vary widely with some states requiring disclosure of deaths within specific timeframes, others requiring disclosure only of certain types of deaths, and still others prohibiting such disclosures. Include any other material facts that a reasonable buyer would want to know when making a purchasing decision, applying the standard that if the seller would want to know this information were they the buyer, it should be disclosed.

## Seller Certification and Execution Requirements

Conclude the disclosure with a comprehensive certification section wherein the seller affirms under penalty of perjury that all information provided is true and correct to the best of their knowledge and belief as of the date of signing. The certification must state that the seller has disclosed all known material defects and conditions affecting the property, that the seller understands their legal obligation to amend this disclosure if they become aware of additional material facts before closing, and that the seller acknowledges this disclosure does not constitute any warranty regarding the property's condition.

Include explicit language stating that this disclosure does not substitute for the buyer's independent inspections, investigations, and due diligence, and that buyers are encouraged to obtain professional inspections of all property systems and components. Provide signature lines for all sellers with printed names and dates, ensuring that every person holding ownership interest in the property signs the disclosure. Include a separate acknowledgment section for buyer signatures confirming receipt of the disclosure, understanding of its contents, and awareness of their right to conduct inspections and investigations.

Specify the timeframe within which buyers must receive this disclosure according to your state's statute, typically ranging from three to ten days before signing the purchase agreement or within a specified period after contract execution. Note the buyer's rights to rescind or renegotiate the purchase agreement if material defects are discovered, and include a clear statement that sellers have an ongoing duty to disclose material changes in property condition that occur between initial disclosure and closing.

## Document Assembly and Quality Review

Assemble the complete Seller Disclosure Statement ensuring every section is thoroughly completed with specific, factual information rather than vague generalizations. Avoid leaving blank spaces that could suggest information was intentionally withheld; instead, mark non-applicable sections as "N/A" or "Not Applicable" with seller initials. Format the document professionally with clear section headings using consistent hierarchy, adequate white space for readability, and uniform formatting throughout. Number all pages and include footer text identifying the property address and disclosure date on each page.

Attach all supporting documentation referenced within the disclosure as exhibits, including inspection reports, repair invoices, contractor evaluations, HOA documents, survey plats, environmental reports, and any other materials that substantiate disclosed conditions or provide additional detail. Organize exhibits logically with a table of contents if numerous documents are attached. Review the completed disclosure for completeness by verifying that every required element under your state's disclosure statute has been addressed, that all statements are clear and specific rather than ambiguous, and that the language used is factual and objective rather than promotional or minimizing.

Verify compliance with state-specific requirements by confirming that all mandatory disclosures have been included, that the document format meets any statutory specifications, and that the certification language tracks the requirements of your jurisdiction's disclosure law. Ensure the final document is ready for seller signature and timely delivery to the buyer within the timeframe required by the purchase agreement and applicable law, recognizing that late delivery may give buyers additional rescission rights or other remedies.

The completed Seller Disclosure Statement should serve as a comprehensive, legally compliant document that fulfills the seller's disclosure obligations while providing buyers with the material information necessary to make an informed purchasing decision. Throughout the drafting process, maintain focus on accuracy, completeness, and clarity, understanding that this document creates legal obligations and potential liabilities for the seller while establishing the foundation for a transparent real estate transaction.
